HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF STEVEN ECHTMAN'S Motion To Strike Or In The Alternative To Tax Costs.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Steven Jay Echtman's motion to strike or, in the alternative, tax costs is denied. Plaintiff's fee waiver, granted on March 2, 2016, does not excuse him from paying defendants' recoverable costs, to which they are entitled as a matter of right because they prevailed on summary judgment. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jun-20-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Steven Echtman (310-962-1021) Plaintiff in propria persona.
Nathaniel Lucey, esq. (408-286-0880) of Ericksen Arbuthnot for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs KOSTOPOULOS LAW GROUP PC, and A. RITA KOSTOPOULOS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is granted.

It is undisputed that Ms. Kostopoulos did not advise Mr. Echtman to delay filing for bankruptcy protection although she was aware that Mr. Echtman had transferred his severance pay to his girlfriend for "safekeeping" several months before he retained Ms. Kostopoulos's services. It is also undisputed that Ms. Kostopoulos did not timely communicate to her client, Mr. Echtman, the settlement offer by the Trustee's counsel during the bankruptcy proceedings. (Echtman depo. 113-114; Lucey Decl. Exh.3; Echtman Decl. Exh. 14). Nevertheless, Defendants are entitled to summary judgment because the undisputed facts show that: (1) Mr. Echtman achieved a better outcome than he would have, had he instructed Ms. Kostopoulos to accept the original settlement offer (which he did not), which would have imposed a three-year refiling bar rather than the two-year bar that was imposed as a result of the stipulation resolving the adversary proceeding; and (2) since Mr. Echtman filed his bankruptcy petition in September 2014, none of his creditors have taken legal action to collect on Mr. Echtman's debts or have communicated to Mr. Echtman their intention of doing so. (UMF 33-35.) Thus even assuming that Mr. Echtman would have been ineligible to obtain a Chapter 7 bankruptcy after the filing bar was lifted in November 2017, Mr. Echtman has failed to show that he has sustained any "actual loss or damage resulting from the attorney's negligence," which is an essential element of his claim for legal malpractice. (Coscia v. McKenna & Cuneo (2001) 25 Cal.4th 1194, 1199.)

"'[D]amages may not be based upon sheer speculation or surmise, and the mere possibility or even probability that damage will result from wrongful conduct does not render it actionable. [Citations.] 'Damage to be subject to a proper award must be such as follows the act complained of as a legal certainty . . . .'" (Ferguson v. Lieff, Cabraser, Heimann & Bernstein (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1037, 1048.) The possibility that Plaintiff's creditors may seek in the future to collect on his past debts is too uncertain to establish that he has sustained damages a cause of action for professional negligence and summary judgment is therefore granted. (See Jordache Enterprises, Inc. v. Brobeck, Phleger & Harrison (1998) 18 Cal.4th 739, 749-750 ["The mere breach of a professional duty, causing only nominal damages, speculative harm, or the threat of future harm-not yet realized-does not suffice to create a cause of action for negligence."];
Philipson & Simon v. Gulsvig (2007) 154 Cal.App.4th 347, 364 ["Generally speaking, to be actionable, harm must constitute something more than nominal damages, speculative harm, or the threat of future harm-not yet realized" (internal quotations omitted)]; Blain v. Doctor's Co. (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1048, 1063 ["There is no cognizable harm attributable to the mere exposure to liability. The exposure must result in compensable detriment."].)

order is signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
